unit.name: Visualization and understanding: saliency, CAM, Grad-CAM, and ViT attention
unit.description: Summarizes common ways to inspect what networks learn, from visualizing filters to attributing class decisions back to pixels or attention maps.
unit.summary: The final segment first connects visualization back to earlier lectures by noting that linear classifiers can be interpreted as class templates and that early CNN filters can likewise be visualized as simple learned edges or shapes, though direct filter visualization becomes harder once hidden layers have many channels [ts=3742s] [ts=3775s] [ts=3808s] [ts=3822s]. It then defines saliency as the gradient of a class score with respect to input pixels, so the largest-magnitude derivatives indicate which pixels most affect the score for a class such as dog [ts=3881s] [ts=3953s] [ts=3979s] [ts=3993s]. For class activation methods, CAM is described as tracing class scores back to spatial feature maps through weighted sums, while Grad-CAM replaces fixed classifier weights with gradient-based weights and a ReLU to highlight positive evidence; for ViTs, the instructor says attention maps already provide a more direct visualization route in pixel space [ts=4048s] [ts=4104s] [ts=4234s] [ts=4264s] [ts=4284s] [ts=4352s] [ts=4381s].

key_points.text: Early CNN filters can be visualized directly and often resemble simple edges, orientations, and basic shapes.
Saliency asks which input pixels matter most for a class prediction.
A saliency map is the gradient of the class score with respect to the input pixels.
CAM localizes class evidence by combining spatial feature maps with class-specific weights.
Grad-CAM uses gradient-derived weights and a ReLU to highlight positive evidence for a class.
ViTs provide attention maps that can be visualized directly over image patches or pixels.

kp_catalog.name: Saliency via input gradients
kp_catalog.description: Saliency maps identify input pixels that most affect a chosen class score by differentiating the score with respect to the input image.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because saliency via input gradients clarifies saliency maps identify input pixels that most affect a chosen class score by differentiating the score with respect to the input image, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: CAM and Grad-CAM
kp_catalog.description: CAM localizes class evidence with weighted feature maps, while Grad-CAM generalizes the idea by deriving weights from gradients at a chosen layer.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because cAM and Grad-CAM clarifies cAM localizes class evidence with weighted feature maps, while Grad-CAM generalizes the idea by deriving weights from gradients at a chosen layer, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: ViT attention visualization
kp_catalog.description: Transformer attention weights can be visualized directly over image patches, giving a more native explanation route than CNN-specific activation methods.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because viT attention visualization extends the core thread to transformer attention weights can be visualized directly over image patches, giving a more native explanation route than CNN-specific activation methods without being required for the main pipeline.

kp_catalog.name: ViT patch tokenization and positional encoding
kp_catalog.description: Representing an image as a sequence of patch tokens, then restoring spatial order with positional embeddings before transformer processing.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because viT patch tokenization and positional encoding formalizes how representing an image as a sequence of patch tokens, then restoring spatial order with positional embeddings before transformer processing, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
